content: 9 min read 22 hours ago -- 💡 Introduction: Why Icon Management is Crucial In every modern front-end project, icons are the backbone of the user experience. They guide intuitively, inform, and beautify the interface. However, poor management of these graphical elements, often seen as a detail, can have a devastating impact on the overall user experience : • Performance: Elevated initial loading time and high size of application bundles. • Customizability: Difficulties in styling or modifying an icon. • Rendering Quality: Lack of sharpness or pixelation on high-resolution screens. Today, I put my front-end architect hat on, and my goal is to guide you towards the best practices in terms of icon management, so you can make conscious technical choices. 1. The Format: SVG vs. Font Icons (Glyph Icons) The first and most fundamental choice concerns the icon format. For years, Font Icons dominated, but more recently SVGs (Scalable Vector Graphics) have taken the “best practice” status. 1.1. Font Icons Icon fonts treat each icon as a typographic character. You can customize the icon via CSS by using text-related CSS rules (color, font-size, etc.) Pros: • Very convenient : Easy to use, well known (icon libraries historically provide font files). • Basic customization: Basic customization like color and size are easy. • High compatibility: They are entirely compatible with older browsers because they all can load fonts. • File size: In the case of complex icons, SVGs get bigger than font icons in file size. Cons: • Not Truly Vector: While they are vectorized to some extent, the rendering is often pixel-based, which can lead to pixelation on edge cases (large screens, big zooms, very small icon). • Single Color: The color style does not allow for complex, multi-colored icons. • File size: You have to be careful when choosing the way you import the fonts. You should only import the icons you are using in your project because it can make your project a lot heavier, which degrades initial loading time. And ideally, you should lazy-load the fonts (which is actually fairly technical to do well, so teams usually load icons at the top of lazy-loaded modules or globally). • Accessibility: Since the icon is a character, it often poses challenges for screen readers. 1.2. SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) SVG is an XML-based vector image format. Pros: • Pure Vector: Perfect quality regardless of size or zoom level. • Styling Flexibility: Full control over styling. Being a DOM element, you can target its paths, circles, and apply complex CSS styles, including multi-color. • Lightweight: For simple icons, the XML code is often lighter than a character in a complete font. Not true for complex icons. • Accessibility: Can be properly marked up with ARIA attributes for screen readers. Cons : • File size : Can be bigger for complex icons • Difficulty of use : Usually judged more complex to manipulate in the code than glyphicons 1.3. Comparison Matrix Press enter or click to view image in full size Font Icons vs. SVG Icons1.4. Conclusion SVG is the modern and undisputed standard for rendering quality and cutomizability. However, if icons are not a big feature in your app and you have basic customization or rendering needs, then you can use glyphicons wihout any issue. Glyphicons are simpler to use and even offer you a better compatibility with old browsers. From a performance perspective, they both can be really fast to load. 2. The Right Way to Handle Glyphicons The main performance flaw when dealing with icon fonts is the all-or-nothing loading model. Since Glyphicons are packaged as a single font file, the browser is forced to download the entire library — often hundreds of unused icons — merely to display the handful you actually need. This wasted bandwidth, particularly on mobile networks, increases latency and download size unnecessarily. To implement the “right way,” you must focus on how you import your icons. 2.1. Import With Granularity Some libraries (Material Symbols for example) expose font files through a customizable url that lets you choose which icons you want to load. This is clearly your best option from a performance perspective. 2.2. Import When it is the Right Time If you decide to load all the icons in a global file when your app starts, make sure that it is not a heavy download. If it is, and is impacting your performances, you should use the lazy-loading capabilities of Angular to make sure the icons are loaded only when needed. 2.3. Import Without HTTP if the Icon is Critical You can never be sure an http request will succeed, neither can you be sure that it will complete quickly. So, if your icon is judged “critical” to the good functioning of your interface, then you should include the font file (*.woff for example) as a base64 string in your @font-face rule, to make sure it is included in the initial bundle. Be careful though, not to overdo it : you don’t want your initial bundle to be crazy heavy. Only critical icons get this treatment ! If your project allows you to use SVGs, inline SVGs are an alternative. They also get included in the initial bundle. 3. The Different Methods of SVG Integration in Angular Now if you decide to go down the SVG route, you have a few ways of doing it. Let’s go through them quickly. 3.1. Static Integration HTML Static integration consists in putting the svg html tag directly in your html code. It is equivalent to the base64 font method we talked about just above : it is very good to ensure the icon gets rendered on the first paint cycle, but it increases the bundle initial size. Use with precaution, only for your critical icons. It has the advantage of fully integrating the SVG structure in your DOM, allowing you to customize it completely via CSS. 3.2. Using the Tag or background-image HTML The SVG is loaded through an http call, and considered as an external resource. Problem: Because the icon is treated as an external resource, the browser has no access to the internal XML code of the SVG. For instance, you cannot use CSS to change the color, which is a major loss of functionality. You can sometimes work around it with a transform property, but you will always be greatly limited. 3.3. The Angular Material Approach (MatIcon API) Angular Material uses an internal SvgIconRegistry to load, cache, and securely inject SVGs. Combined with a MatIconcomponent to handle the DOM rendering efficiently, it is a performant and effective way to load your SVGs. Your SVG structure ends up directly in the DOM, so you can customize it easily and it is highly accessible. You get reusability and an efficient tree-shaking because you are using an Angular component to render the SVG. And as a bonus, the svg file will be lazy-loaded and cached. The Catch: If you are developing a custom UI library or if your project doesn’t use the rest of the Angular Material Design System, adding that full dependency solely for icons is a bad idea. We want a modular solution. Luckily, we can create our own custom solution for this technical use case, and it is quite easy to setup and maintain. Let’s dive into it. 4. The Best Practice: Building Your Own High-Performance SVG Loader I will market this as being the best practice but as you know, it always depends on what you are trying to do. What we want to do here is to replicate the logic of a minimalistic SvgIconRegistry from Angular Material, to load an SVG icon with cache management. We also want to render our SVGs via a component, because we would like to keep the lazy-loading and the tree-shaking to ensure optimal performance. Lastly, we want the SVG to be inlined in the DOM so we can fully customize it with CSS when needed. 4.1. 🔒 The Security Imperative: Sanitization This is the most critical step. To inject raw HTML/SVG content into the DOM from a dynamic source (HTTP), we must use the [innerHTML] host binding. ⚠️ The Danger: If an SVG is loaded from a malicious source and contains malicious JavaScript (), [innerHTML] will execute it. The solution is to use Angular’s DomSanitizer, which cleans (sanitizes) the content before injection. However, Angular's sanitizer is very strict and often removes useful SVG attributes. We must therefore explicitly tell Angular to trust our icon source: this.domSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(svgContent)By using bypassSecurityTrustHtml, we, as developers, vouch that the content is safe. This is mandatory for the injection to work without being blocked by Angular. However, before calling bypassSecurityTrustHtml you must make sure that the SVG can be trusted. If the source of the svg file is you, or one of your company’s servers then you can just call the bypass method. But if the svg files come from an external source, you should ALWAYS add code to check the integrity of the svg before calling bypassSecurityTrustHtml . 4.2. The Code The system relies on two classes: SvgIconLoader (the data service) and SvgIcon (the display component). 4.2.1. The Service: SvgIconLoader (Icon loading, Cache management) // SvgIconLoader.ts@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) export class SvgIconLoader { private cache = new Map(); // ... HttpClient, DomSanitizer injections ... public getIcon(name: string): Observable { if (this.cache.has(name)) { return of(this.cache.get(name)!); // 1. Instantly returns from cache } const svgSourceUrl = `assets/icons/${name}.svg`; return this.http.get(svgSourceUrl, { responseType: 'text' }).pipe( map(svgFile => /* ... sanitization ... */), tap(safeSvg => { this.cache.set(name, safeSvg); // 2. Caches after loading }) ); } } This service assumes you store your SVG icons in your assets folders, and fetches them by file name. I added a minimalist cache implementation, which guarantees that for each icon, only one HTTP request will be sent, regardless of how many times that icon is used in the application (make sure it is injected through your app root injector). 4.2.2. The Component: SvgIcon (Reactive Display, Lazy-loading, Tree-shaking) // SvgIcon.ts @Component({ selector: 'lib-svg-icon', template: '', host: { '[innerHTML]': 'this.svgContent()' // The secured injection point } }) export class SvgIcon { readonly name = input.required(); private svgContent$ = toObservable(this.name).pipe( distinctUntilChanged(), // 1. Avoids unnecessary reloads switchMap((name) => this.svgIconLoader.getIcon(name)) // 2. Asks for the icon, Cancels old requests ); protected readonly svgContent = toSignal(this.svgContent$, { initialValue: /* ... default SafeHtml value ... */ }); }The component is in charge of triggering the http call to get the icon it needs. Which means your app only fetches the icon via HTTP when it is needed by a SvgIcon component instantiated in the DOM (lazy loading). It prevents you from downloading unused icons on your user’s browser. • The use of switchMap is critical. If the user switches icons rapidly (e.g., fast view changes), switchMap cancels obsolete HTTP requests, improving network performance. • The integration of Signals via toSignal makes the component extremely efficient, as it only refreshes when the SVG content changes. • The [innerHTML] host binding uses the secured value (SafeHtml) to inline the SVG code directly into the host element . This way we keep the SVG fully customizable. 4.3. 🧑‍💻 How to Use It Thanks to this architecture, usage is minimal and expressive: You can now load a SVG icon, just by passing its file name in the name input property of the SvgIcon component. The icon can be styled d
